title: Function notation word problems
description: Interpret expressions with function notation in terms of the context that the function models.

assessment_items.question_content:
Copacabana beach in Rio de Janeiro is one of the most popular beaches in the world.

$P(t)$ models the number of people at the beach, $t$ hours past midnight on a specific day.

**What does the statement $P(10)=N$ mean?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
Remember that *$P(t)$ denotes the number of people $P$ at the Copacabana beach, $t$ hours past midnight.*

This means that $P(10)$ is the number of people that were at the beach at $10{:}00\text{ a.m.}$

Therefore, $P(10)=N$ means that the number of people that were at the beach at $10{:}00\text{ a.m.}$ is equal to $N$.

**In other words, the statement means that *there were $N$ people at the beach at $10{:}00\text{ a.m.}$***

choices.content: There were $N$ people at the beach at $10{:}00\text{ a.m.}$
There were $10$ people at the beach at $N$ hours past midnight.
There was an equal number of people at the beach at $10{:}00\text{ a.m.}$ and at $N$ hours past midnight.

assessment_items.question_content:
Tessa's class had a math exam where the grades were between $0$ and $10$.

$N(g)$ models the number of students whose grade on the exam was $g$.

**What does the statement $N(8)>2\!\cdot\!N(5)$ mean?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
Remember that *$N(g)$ denotes the number of students $N$ whose grade on the exam was $g$*

This means that $N(8)$ and $N(5)$ are the number of students whose grade on the exam was $8$ and $5$, respectively.

Therefore, $N(8)>2\!\cdot\!N(5)$ means that the number of students whose grade was $8$ is greater than the number of students whose grade was $5$ multiplied by $2$.

**In other words, the statement means that *the number of students whose grade was $8$ is more than twice the number of students whose grade was $5$.***

choices.content: The number of students whose grade was $8$ is more than twice the number of students whose grade was $5$.
There are $8$ students whose grade was higher than twice the grade of another group of $5$ students.
The number of students whose grade was higher than $5$ is greater than the number of students whose grade was $5$ or lower.

assessment_items.question_content:
Baby Amelia's parents measure her height every month.

$H(t)$ models Amelia's height (in centimeters) when she was $t$ months old.

**What does the statement $H(30)=H(25)+5$ mean?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
Remember that *$H(t)$ denote Amelia's height $H$ when she was $t$ months old.*

This means that $H(30)$ and $H(25)$ are Amelia's height when she was $30$ months old and $25$ months old, respectively.

Therefore, $H(30)=H(25)+5$ means that Ameila's height when she was $30$ months old is equal to her height when she was $25$ months old plus $5$ centimeters.

**In other words, the statement means that *when Amelia was $30$ months old, she was $5$ centimeters taller than when she was $25$ months old.***

choices.content: When Amelia was $30$ months old, she was $5$ centimeters taller than when she was $25$ months old.
It took Amelia $5$ months to grow from a height of $25$ centimeters to a height of $30$ centimeters.
The sum of Amelia's heights at $5$ months and $25$ months old is equal to her height at $30$ months old.

assessment_items.question_content:
Baby Kana's parents measure her height every month.

$H(t)$ models Kana's height (in centimeters) when she was $t$ months old.

**What does the statement $H(160)=150$ mean?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
Remember that *$H(t)$ denote Kana's height $H$ when she was $t$ months old.*

This means that $H(160)$ is Kana's height when she was $160$ months old.

Therefore, $H(160)=150$ means that Kana's height when she was $160$ months old is equal to $150$ centimeters.

**In other words, the statement means that *when Kana was $160$ months old, her height was equal to $150$ centimeters.***

choices.content: When Kana was $160$ months old, her height was equal to $150$ centimeters.
When Kana was $150$ months old, her height was equal to $160$ centimeters.
Kana had the same height at $150$ months old and at $160$ months old.

assessment_items.question_content:
"Princess Tower" in Dubai is considered the world's tallest residential building, towering to $101$ floors!

$T(n)$ models the number of tenants living on floor $n$ of the tower.

**What does the statement $T(60)>T(10)+T(30)$ mean?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
Remember that *$T(n)$ denotes the number of tenants $T$ living on floor $n$.*

This means that $T(60)$, $T(10)$, and $T(30)$ are the number of tenants on floors $60$, $10$, and $30$, respectively.

Therefore, $T(60)>T(10)+T(30)$ means that the number of tenants on floor $60$ is greater than the sum of the number of tenants on floors $10$ and $30$.

**In other words, the statement means that *there are more tenants on floor $60$ than there are tenants on floors $10$ and $30$ combined.***

choices.content: There are more tenants on floor $60$ than there are tenants on floors $10$ and $30$ combined.
The height of floor $60$ is greater than the sum of the heights of floors $10$ and $30$.
The number of floors with $60$ tenants is greater than the number of floors with either $10$ or $30$ tenants.

assessment_items.question_content:
"Princess Tower" in Dubai is considered the world's tallest residential building, towering to $101$ floors!

$T(n)$ models the number of tenants living on floor $n$ of the tower.

**What does the statement $T(20)=T(c)$ mean?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
Remember that *$T(n)$ denotes the number of tenants $T$ living on floor $n$.*

This means that $T(20)$ is the number of tenants on floor $20$ and $T(c)$ is the number of tenants on floor $c$.

Therefore, $T(20)=T(c)$ means that the number of tenants on floor $20$ is equal to the number of tenants on floor $c$.

**In other words, the statement means that *floor $20$ and floor $c$ have the same number of tenants.***

choices.content: Floor $20$ and floor $c$ have the same number of tenants.
The number of tenants on floor $c$ is equal to $20$.
The number of tenants on floor $20$ is equal to $c$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Alek went for a walk.

$D(t)$ models the distance Alek walked (in kilometers) after $t$ hours.

**What does the statement $D(0.5)<D(1)-D(0.5)$ mean?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
Remember that *$D(t)$ denotes the distance Alek walked $D$ after walking for $t$ hours.*

This means that $D(0.5)$ and $D(1)$ are the distances Alek walked after walking for $0.5$ hour and $1$ hour, respectively.

Therefore, $D(0.5)<D(1)-D(0.5)$ means that the distance Alek walked after $0.5$ hour is less than the distance he walked between $0.5$ hour after he started and $1$ hour after he started.

**In other words, the statement means that *the distance Alek walked during the first half hour is shorter than the distance he walked during the following half hour.***

choices.content: The distance Alek walked during the first half hour is shorter than the distance he walked during the following half hour.
The time it took Alek to walk the first half kilometer is shorter than the time it took him to walk the following half kilometer.
The distance Alek has walked after walking for an hour is greater than the distance he has walked after half an hour.

assessment_items.question_content:
Aiden is a taxi driver.

$M(n)$ models Aiden's fee (in dollars) for his $n^\text{th}$ drive on a certain day.

**What does the statement $M(8)<M(4)$ mean?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
Remember that *$M(n)$ denotes Aiden's fee $M$ for his $n^\text{th}$ drive.*

This means that $M(8)$ and $M(4)$ are the fees for Aiden's $8^\text{th}$ drive and his $4^\text{th}$ drive, respectively.
Therefore, $M(8)<M(4)$ means that *Aiden earned less money for his $8^\text{th}$ than he earned for his $4^\text{th}$ drive*.

choices.content: Aiden earned less money for his $8^\text{th}$ than he earned for his $4^\text{th}$ drive.
Aiden earned $\$8$ for a drive that was earlier than a drive for which he earned $\$4$.
Aiden earned $\$8$ for fewer drives than the number of drives for which he earned $\$4$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Jayce is a taxi driver.

$M(n)$ models Jayce's fee (in dollars) for his $n^\text{th}$ drive on a certain day.

**What does the statement $M(10)=K$ mean?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
Remember that *$M(n)$ denotes Jayce's fee $M$ for his $n^\text{th}$ drive.*

This means that $M(10)$ is Jayce's fee for his $10^\text{th}$ drive.
Therefore, $M(10)=K$ means that *Jayce's fee for his $10^\text{th}$ drive is equal to $K$ dollars.*

choices.content: Jayce's fee for his $10^\text{th}$ drive is equal to $K$ dollars.
Jayce's fee for his $K^\text{th}$ drive is equal to $\$10$.
Jayce's fees for his $K^\text{th}$ drive and his $10^\text{th}$ drive are equal.

assessment_items.question_content:
Kaori is taking a free-throw.

$H(d)$ models the basketball's height (in meters) at a horizontal distance of $d$ meters from Kaori.

**What does the statement $H(R)=4$ mean?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
Remember that *$H(d)$ denotes the basketball's height $H$ at a horizontal distance of $d$ meters from Kaori.*

This means that $H(R)$ is the ball's height at a horizontal distance of $R$ meters.

Therefore, $H(R)=4$ means that the ball's height at a horizontal distance of $R$ meters is equal to $4$ meters

**In other words, the statement means that *at a horizontal distance of $R$ meters from Kaori, the ball's height was equal to $4$ meters.***

choices.content: At a horizontal distance of $R$ meters from Kaori, the ball's height was equal to $4$ meters.
At a horizontal distance of $4$ meters from Kaori, the ball's height was equal to $R$ meters.
The ball was at the same height at the horizontal distances of $4$ meters and $R$ meters.

assessment_items.question_content:
Aria walked down a trail into a valley.

$V(d)$ models Aria's vertical distance from the top of the valley (in kilometers) after walking $d$ kilometers along the trail.

**What does the statement $V(3)<3$ mean?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
Remember that *$V(d)$ denotes Aria's vertical distance from the top of the valley $V$ after she has walked $d$ kilometers along the trail.*

This means that $V(3)$ is Aria's vertical distance from the top of the valley after she has walked $3\text{ km}$.

Therefore, $V(3)<3$ means that Aria's vertical distance from the top of the valley after she has walked $3\text{ km}$ is less than $3\text{ km}$.

**In other words, the statement means that *when Aria had walked $3\text{ km}$ along the trail, her vertical distance from the top of the valley was less than $3\text{ km}$.***

choices.content: When Aria had walked $3\text{ km}$ along the trail, her vertical distance from the top of the valley was less than $3\text{ km}$.
When Aria's vertical distance from the top of the valley was $3\text{ km}$, the distance she had walked along the trail was less than $3\text{ km}$.
The time it took Aria to walk $3\text{ km}$ is shorter than the time it took her to be at a vertical distance of $3\text{ km}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Erica walked down a trail into a valley.

$V(d)$ models Erica's vertical distance from the top of the valley (in kilometers) after walking $d$ kilometers along the trail.

**What does the statement $V(2)=T$ mean?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
Remember that *$V(d)$ denotes Erica's vertical distance from the top of the valley $V$ after she has walked $d$ kilometers along the trail.*

This means that $V(2)$ is Erica's vertical distance from the top of the valley after she has walked $2\text{ km}$.

Therefore, $V(2)=T$ means that Erica's vertical distance from the top of the valley after she has walked $2\text{ km}$ is equal to $T$ kilometers.

**In other words, the statement means that *after she had walked $2\text{ km}$ along the trail, Erica's vertical distance from the top of the valley was equal to $T$ kilometers.***

choices.content: After she had walked $2\text{ km}$ along the trail, Erica's vertical distance from the top of the valley was equal to $T$ kilometers.
After she had walked $T$ kilometers along the trail, Erica's vertical distance from the top of the valley was equal to $2\text{ km}$
Erica's vertical distance from the top of the valley was the same after she had walked $2\text{ km}$ and after she had walked $T$ kilometers.
